what_it_does: Enumerates Open WebUI 0.9.5 pluggable capabilities—web search, content loaders, document extraction, image generation, STT/TTS, code execution, vector databases, LLM providers, tools/functions/filters/pipes/skills, MCP servers, automations, OAuth, knowledge/memory, UI branding, and multi-user features—and maps each to current enablement status, cost, local/cloud dependency, and notes for this substrate.
observations: Status legend uses ✓ for enabled, ◇ for available but not enabled, key for needing an API key, and local for no external account.
Most providers are not enabled; only built-in document extraction, Whisper STT, ChromaDB, and local model endpoints are marked live.
Tim has API keys for Tavily, Firecrawl, Brave, Perplexity, OpenAI, and Google Workspace, but handover is pending.
Qdrant and pgvector are already running on the machine, yet ChromaDB is the current default vector store.
The file recommends a four-tier rollout: free/local first, keyed providers next, vector DB switch, then MCP wiring.
Includes community sources (openwebui.com/tools, functions, prompts) and new 0.9.x features such as Skills, scheduled automations, and MCP OAuth.
Ends with an 'Open at this catalog' section listing known gaps and unresolved questions.
standouts: The file is explicitly a menu/catalog, not live configuration; live state lives in docker inspect and the Admin Settings UI.
Strong recommendation to switch the vector DB from ChromaDB to the already-running Qdrant instance.
MCP integration is flagged as the architecturally interesting next step because multiple MCP processes already run on the machine.
A tiered enablement plan separates immediate no-wait items from key-dependent and architectural next steps.
conventions: Uses wikilink-style cross-references such as [[_operations-index]] and relative paths like [[../system/architecture]].
Tables use a consistent status/cost/notes or status/notes structure.
Recommendation callouts are bolded (e.g., 'AI-first search', 'Best free option').
Records key availability in a dedicated date-stamped section.
Maintains a provisional 'Open at this catalog' TODO list for gaps.
concerns: Many 'Tim has key' claims rest on a single 2026-05-28 conversation and await actual handover.
Static catalog will drift as toggles and keys change; it does not auto-sync with live container state.
Image generation table lists Google Imagen / Gemini Image twice with slightly different wording.
'External (custom URL)' rows do not specify the expected endpoint schema.
notes: A static capability menu for Open WebUI 0.9.5 with enablement status, key inventory, and a staged rollout plan.
questions: Which specific Google API products are enabled and which exact keys exist?
Is Anthropic direct available via existing Claude Code access or a separate API key?
What is the exact WSL/backend setup for the Conduit iOS client and Open WebUI Desktop v0.0.20?
Which vector database is currently active in the live Open WebUI container?
purpose_vs_actual: Stated purpose is a comprehensive enumeration of pluggable Open WebUI extras; actual content matches and additionally provides key inventory, recommendations, and staged rollout tiers.
apparent_themes: Integration architecture reuse (MCP, existing vector stores)
intention_signals: Provide a single reference that lets Tim decide what to turn on and in what order, while recording which API keys are already available and which gaps remain.
novelty: Treats an Open WebUI deployment as a substrate with a 'menu' of optional modules, explicitly separating catalog state from runtime state and staging activation by dependency on keys and existing infrastructure.
contribution: A structured, status-annotated inventory of Open WebUI extension points paired with a concrete staged enablement plan and a key-handover tracker.
summary_for_embedding: This file is a catalog of Open WebUI 0.9.5 pluggable extras—search providers, content loaders, document extractors, image/TTS/STT/code/vector DB/LLM providers, tools, MCP, automations, OAuth, knowledge, UI, and multi-user features—annotated with current enablement status, cost, key availability, and a tiered rollout plan.
intention_for_embedding: To serve as a decision menu and staged activation roadmap for extending the local Open WebUI instance without confusing catalog state with live configuration.
